data.title: Hong Kong slams attempts to ‘smear’ government ahead of Jimmy Lai mitigation
data.description:
A top Hong Kong official has condemned attempts by external forces and “anti-China absconders” to “smear” the government ahead of Jimmy Lai Chee-ying’s mitigation hearing, saying the former media boss’ conviction showed that any plots to undermine national security will fail.
Secretary for Constitutional and Mainland Affairs Erick Tsang Kwok-wai said on Saturday that such smear campaigns during Lai’s national security trial were proof that Hong Kong still faced risks that could not be taken lightly.
“Lai’s conviction showed that any intent to damage the safety, unity and prosperity of the nation will not achieve its aims, will not succeed, and will not hamper the nation’s way to strength and national rejuvenation,” Tsang said.
Lai, founder of the now-defunct Apple Daily tabloid, was found guilty last month of two charges of conspiracy to collude with foreign forces and one count of conspiracy to print and distribute seditious articles in a landmark High Court trial that ran for more than 1½ years.
A panel of three judges, hand-picked by the city’s leader to adjudicate national security cases, found that Lai had used his newspaper and international connections to push for foreign intervention and sanctions against the Hong Kong and central governments.
The 78-year-old activist faces a minimum sentence of 10 years behind bars and up to life imprisonment. Mitigation will begin on Monday, with four days set aside for the hearing.

Tsang said external forces and their agents had used “soft resistance” tactics during Lai’s trial in an attempt to incite hatred among residents against the local and central governments.
“During Lai’s trial, external hostile forces tried to smear and attack the Hong Kong government, while anti-China absconders, under the protection of foreign forces, had repeatedly tried to damage national security,” Tsang said.
Speaking at a graduation ceremony for a certificate programme for national security tutors, Tsang highlighted the impact of two sets of national security legislation.
“The Hong Kong national security law and the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance have provided the most robust foundation and protection for long-term economic prosperity and development in Hong Kong,” Tsang said.


Beijing promulgated the national security law in 2020 in response to the months-long social unrest that had rocked Hong Kong the previous year, while the city’s Legislative Council unanimously passed the home-grown Safeguarding National Security Ordinance in 2024.
Dozens of people were already queuing at West Kowloon Court on Saturday afternoon to attend Monday’s mitigation hearing.
Among those at the front of the queue was a retiree surnamed Lam, who had also waited in the past for previous court hearings involving Lai.
Fearing her spot could be taken by “queuing syndicates”, the 70-year-old has queued since 4am on Friday, as she hoped to see Lai in person. Lam said she had brought more clothes and supplies with her to make the wait more bearable.

data.title: Singapore steps up migrant worker reforms amid calls for deeper change
data.description:
The life of a migrant worker in Singapore is bittersweet.
Ramesh, 29, came to the city state in search of a good job. He hails from Tamil Nadu, India, and earns just over S$600 (US$466) a month working as a technician at a large multinational firm.
While this is double what he made in India and has helped put his sisters through college there, it is only about 10 per cent of the S$5,775 nominal median gross monthly income of Singaporeans.
Grappling with an ageing population and a low fertility rate, Singapore has stepped up efforts to improve conditions facing its many low-paid migrant workers, also known as work permit holders.
But a lack of space and high business costs are obstacles to improving their lives.

Ramesh, a pseudonym, shares a dormitory room with 15 others. He commutes in the backs of open lorries, a practice non-governmental organisations want banned due to fatalities and injuries caused by traffic accidents. The government has responded with measures such as requiring lorries to be fitted with speed limiters.
Ramesh looks forward to the balmy evenings after work. On Sundays, he brews masala tea to bring to the sprawling compound of a nearby worker recreation centre. It’s a sanctuary from his daily grind, a small world of its own apart from the big city beyond.
Ramesh lives a frugal existence, allocating S$150 for meals, S$50 for phone bills and saving S$400 to send to his family in India each month. It’s a lifestyle that thousands of others pursue to improve their lot.
Singapore has long relied on foreign workers, who account for about 40 per cent of its total population, but the paid migrant workers who make up most of this group have received little public appreciation until recently.
Drawing lessons from the Covid-19 pandemic that paralysed economic activities when the virus spread through overcrowded dorms, the government has rolled out a slew of new initiatives.
The first government-built dorm opened last month, part of a plan to set higher standards for safety and living conditions, better healthcare access for migrant workers and improved recreation centres.
“We have made significant progress in the areas of housing, healthcare and recreation since we launched the multi-year road map in 2021,” Manpower Minister Tan See Leng said at an event to mark International Migrants Day on December 14, telling migrants, “You build the foundations of where we live, work and play and you keep our towns running every day.”

Under Singapore’s pyramidlike, highly managed system of work passes, work permit holders are at the base, as the lowest paid and lowest skilled, while employment pass holders, who are white-collar workers, sit at the apex.
“Singaporeans are more tolerant of the low-wage workers because they know that they are doing jobs that Singaporeans do not want to do,” said Ethan Guo, executive director from Transient Workers Count Too, a non-profit working to improve conditions of migrant workers.
“They are a necessity because who else is going to take away your garbage? Who else is going to do the construction work in the hot sun?”
The employment pass holders, on the other hand, are people who can do the jobs that Singaporeans can also do, according to Guo.
“Those kinds of foreigners tend to be seen more in a negative light,” he said.
At 1.18 million, work permit holders account for more than 70 per cent of Singapore’s 1.59 million foreign workforce, toiling on construction sites, roadworks and shipyards under the blistering sun, cleaning streets and hauling away trash.
Their number has steadily risen, especially in the last three years, while employment pass holders declined slightly towards the end of the same period, according to government data.

“If they are not here, our construction work will stop, totally stop, and you cannot see buildings coming up,” said Lee Kay Chai, president of the Singapore Contractors Association, which recently hosted a thank you party for migrants in construction.
“The Singapore way of life and economy completely depends on access to large numbers of work permit holders. This is because we cannot meet economic demands without them,” said Walter Theseira, an associate professor at the Singapore University of Social Sciences.
The demand for migrant workers has surged in the post-pandemic economic recovery.
To cope with this boom in demand, in July Singapore removed a limit on the number of years work permit holders can work in the city state.
“The overall situation has been improving for workers in some ways, but change has been slow and not necessarily in the areas that are meaningful,” Guo said.
“In the bigger picture, it is neither sustainable nor desirable for Singapore to continue with a model of progress that is built on cheap labour.”
As for Ramesh, he continues to brew his masala tea while dreaming of a better life.
With a diploma earned through part-time study during weekends in Singapore and a neatly prepared resume, he is now searching for a better-paying job in his adopted home.

data.title: China calls on US to form united front against Japanese militarism
data.description:
China has called on the US to work together to prevent a resurgence of militarism in Japan, with Beijing signalling that more sanctions could be in the pipeline for Tokyo’s defence industry.
“Preventing militarism from harming the world again is in the common interest of both our countries [China and the United States],” Liu Pengyu, spokesman for the Chinese embassy in Washington, said in an article published in US-based Executive Intelligence Review earlier this week.
“We should rise above differences, cooperate with each other, and live up to our obligations as major countries ... and thwart any plot to revive the spectre of militarism.”
China and Japan have been embroiled in a diplomatic row since November when Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi suggested that a Taiwan contingency could warrant a Japanese military response, which Beijing views as crossing its red line.
Beijing has repeatedly said Taiwan is the core of its core issues. It views the island as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including Japan and the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
In the months since making the suggestion, Takaichi has refused to retract her comment and Beijing has taken a series of diplomatic and economic retaliatory actions.


Beijing has also stepped up warnings about Japan’s military expansion and renewed debate over its capacity to acquire nuclear weapons.
On Tuesday, China imposed a ban on exports of dual-use commercial and military goods to end users with links to the Japanese military. It was followed the next day by an anti-dumping investigation into a chipmaking material from Japan.
The precision of China’s export controls is aimed at Japan’s long-standing practice of “blurring the line between civilian and military sectors”, according to an article published on Friday by Yuyuan Tantian, a social media account run by state broadcaster CCTV.
“Tokyo has deliberately obscured its activities by embedding defence production within its civilian industrial system, with many key defence programmes planned by the defence ministry but outsourced to private contractors,” it said.
This system, the article argued, underpinned right-wing Japanese politicians’ claims that “a Taiwan contingency is a Japan contingency”, and reflected their ambition to resurrect militarism.
“China is now moving to cut off this support at its root,” it said, adding that China was using internationally recognised rules to contain Japan’s “remilitarisation” schemes and defend its national security and interests.
“China’s focus has extended beyond any single statement by Japan to the systemic and fundamental issues that have long persisted in the country.”


Beijing is also unnerved by the escalating nuclear ambition of Japan’s right-wing forces.
A senior Japanese official said last month that he believed Japan should have nuclear weapons, but Tokyo denied that the claim represented Japan’s official position.
In a report released earlier this week, a number of Chinese bodies called for the international community to “take concrete and strong measures” against these ambitions.
Japan “may already have produced weapons-grade plutonium in secret” and “has the technological and economic capabilities to achieve nuclear armament in a short period of time”, said the report, produced jointly by the China Arms Control and Disarmament Association and the Nuclear Strategic Planning Research Institute, a think tank affiliated with China National Nuclear Corporation.
According to Japanese media, Takaichi is planning to visit the US as early as March to meet US President Donald Trump on what would be her first trip to the country since taking office in October.
With a slim legislative majority, she was also weighing a snap general election to capitalise on her government’s approval ratings of around 70 per cent, media reports said.
An early election as well as a stronger parliamentary position could help solidify her mandate and help her navigate strained ties with China.

data.title: Hong Kong’s Legco to discuss post-Tai Po fire support but question session cancelled
data.description:
Hong Kong’s newly elected lawmakers will discuss a government motion to strengthen support for Wang Fuk Court fire victims at their first chamber meeting next Wednesday, but will skip a regular question session for officials.
The Legislative Council chamber meeting for legislators, who assumed their posts on January 1, will also debate a lawmaker’s motion that calls for a review of the city’s building maintenance system and a crackdown on bid rigging in renovation projects.
Legco announced the agenda in the late hours of Friday, saying the first chamber would hear the government motion titled “support and rebuilding work after the fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po” by Chief Secretary Eric Chan Kwok-ki.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu will also give an opening speech at the chamber meeting.
The catastrophic 43-hour blaze at the residential complex in Tai Po, which started on November 26, claimed at least 160 lives and displaced nearly 5,000 residents.
The estate, which had been undergoing renovations since July last year, was covered in scaffolding and mesh. Some scaffold net samples collected after the fire failed fire-retardant tests.
According to the paper uploaded to the Legco website, the motion centres on emergency financial help for residents, accommodation arrangements and long-term solutions.
It also asks lawmakers to discuss with the government ways to advance legislative reviews to improve the system and close loopholes.
“To ensure the meeting can fully focus on this important motion, after consultation with government authorities, the Legislative Council president has decided that no question time will be arranged for this meeting,” the Legco secretariat said in an announcement.
The current Legco president is Starry Lee Wai-king, who won the election on Thursday.
The secretariat added that Lee decided to extend the debate time for this motion to allow all members who wish to speak to do so, given the grave public concerns over the support and reconstruction work following the fire, and considering the broad scope of issues involved.
Each member can speak once, with a five minute time limit.

Lee also decided that the meeting would handle only one motion proposed by lawmaker Mark Chong Ho-fung, with four hours allocated for the discussion.
Chong’s motion – “improving the management of building maintenance works” – urges the government to reform the building maintenance regime and procedures, and rigorously crack down on bid-rigging in building maintenance works.
It also calls on authorities to enhance the specification of construction material requirements, step up inspections of building maintenance sites and strengthen the monitoring of the construction process.
The city’s leader had promised to take action at the first meeting of the new Legco and push through systemic reforms to close loopholes in the construction sector’s practices, which have come under heavy scrutiny in the aftermath of the Wang Fuk Court fire tragedy.
He also ordered the creation of the committee in early December to identify the cause of the blaze and initiate “systemic reforms”. The investigation report is expected to be submitted in nine months.
Authorities have so far arrested at least 23 people on suspicion of offences such as manslaughter and corruption in connection with the fire.

data.title: 1 dead in UK, 100,000 French homes without power as storms batter Europe
data.description:
A man was killed after a tree fell on a caravan in England after record winds brought by Storm Goretti, as 100,000 homes in France were still without power on Saturday.
Some 15 people have died in weather-related accidents this week across Europe as gale-force winds and storms caused travel mayhem, shut schools, and cut power to hundreds of thousands in freezing temperatures.
The storm barrelled through southwestern Cornwall and parts of Wales overnight Thursday to Friday, with gusts of up to 160km per hour (100 miles per hour) downing trees and leaving tens of thousands of homes without power.

UK police said a man was found dead in the town of Helston in Cornwall on Friday after a tree fell onto a caravan.
“Tragically, a man aged in his 50s was located deceased within the caravan,” Devon and Cornwall police said in a statement.
Most of the United Kingdom remained under a weather warning for snow and ice on Saturday, the Met Office national weather agency said, warning that black ice could cause “disruption” in Scotland and northern England.
Heavy snowfall followed by the storm meant that some 250 schools in Scotland were closed for much of the first week back after the Christmas break.
Around 28,000 homes were still without power at the start of the weekend in southwestern England and the Midlands, according to the network operator National Grid.

Storm Goretti also ploughed through other parts of northern Europe, with nearly 100,000 homes in France still without power on Saturday morning.
The winds felled trees, with at least one crashing on homes in the country’s Seine-Maritime region, without injuries, authorities said.
Meanwhile, long-distance rail traffic slowly resumed on Saturday in northern Germany, after being completely suspended on Friday due to another storm named Elli, rail operator Deutsche Bahn said.
In the far north of the country, the port city of Hamburg, heavily affected by a large amount of snow, remained particularly affected by the disruptions, it added.
A number of rail services would still not be restored on Saturday, notably those linking Hamburg to Copenhagen, Amsterdam and Hanover.
Services from Hamburg to the western Ruhr region or to Berlin were expected to be restored over the course of Saturday, it said.

data.title: Why Bad Bunny’s Grammy nominations mark a symbolic moment for Latinos
data.description:
The Puerto Rican superstar Bad Bunny has redefined what it means to be a global giant – and he may once again make history at the 2026 Grammy Awards.
The artist, born Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio, is up for six awards at the February 1 show, becoming the first Spanish-language artist to be nominated simultaneously for album, song and record of the year.
His critically acclaimed album, Debi Tirar Mas Fotos, is only the second Spanish-language record to be nominated for album of the year. The first? Well, that also belonged to Bad Bunny, 2022’s Un Verano Sin Ti.
Win or lose, experts say that Bad Bunny’s Grammy nominations mark a symbolic moment for Latinos. Just a week later, after all, he will headline the Super Bowl half-time show.
Vanessa Diaz, associate professor of Chicano and Latino studies at Loyola Marymount University in Los Angeles, California, and co-author of P FKN R: How Bad Bunny Became the Global Voice of Puerto Rican Resistance, says Bad Bunny’s nods extend beyond his own art and serve as a “very welcome recognition of Latin music that is growing”.
Albert Laguna, associate professor of American studies and ethnicity, race and migration at Yale University in Connecticut, adds: “Music from the Spanish-speaking Caribbean has been shaping global music tastes since the 19th century. Bad Bunny is another link in a much longer chain of the popularity of Caribbean music on a global stage.”

Much of this music – particularly Latin trap and reggaeton, the genres Bad Bunny got his start in and continues to use in his new work – has been historically criminalised in Puerto Rico, not unlike hip-hop in the US.
Reggaeton in particular, Diaz points out, “comes from the most marginalised communities in Puerto Rico. And so, the fact that Bad Bunny is receiving nominations in three main categories, and this is an artist who came up with trap … is the most groundbreaking thing about the entire situation.”
Petra Rivera-Rideau, associate professor of American studies at Wellesley College in Massachusetts and co-author of P FKN R, says that element is particularly noteworthy because institutions often ignore marginalised genres – including at the Latin Grammys, a sister award show to the Grammys.
A victory in the major categories could have “profound, symbolic meaning”, she says. But with a caveat: “I’m interested to see if this is going to open doors for other people.”
After all, Bad Bunny himself is not immune to the Recording Academy’s institutional biases: he already has three career Grammys, but all have been in música urbana, or Latin urban, categories – even though he is the most-streamed artist on the planet.
Across Debi Tirar Mas Fotos, Bad Bunny and his producers weave traditional Puerto Rican folkloric styles into a hyper-contemporary context. While Bad Bunny’s previous albums also fused different genres – including bossa nova, mambo, rock, merengue and more – this album’s melange was more home-grown.

Laguna sees Debi Tirar Mas Fotos as a direct challenge to the prevailing “formula for global pop stardom”, which he describes as an artist making it locally, gaining traction and then “watering down” their sound into something commercial and palatable for a global audience.
“Bad Bunny went in the opposite direction. It’s his most Puerto Rican album ever,” says Laguna. He hopes it will communicate to other artists that they, too, can look to their ancestry and history for artmaking.
“There’s so much amazing Latin music that has been overlooked, and that’s part of what is so beautiful about this moment,” Diaz says. “And that’s why it feels like a win for all Latinos.”
The timing of the album’s release and recognition, too, feels consequential, Diaz adds. “The US has a history of othering Latinos, othering the Spanish language … we’re in a moment where that feels extremely acute. For a community that is being targeted on such a deep level, it is a little bit of light, a little bit of faith that we can still carve out our place here.”
Latinos and the Spanish-speaking community in the US have grown increasingly wary amid growing anti-immigrant sentiment and raids, as US President Donald Trump’s immigration policies and executive actions have vastly expanded who is eligible for deportation, and routine hearings have turned into deportation traps for migrants.

If Bad Bunny does win, Diaz says, it will be “akin to Halle Berry being the first black woman to win an Oscar. That was a watershed moment. Or Rita Moreno being the first Latina to win.”
Beyond that, Laguna says the politics of the album are not exclusive to Puerto Rican or even Latino identity – “the lyrics on this album align with global struggles”, he says.
Rivera-Rideau says one of the reasons Debi Tirar Mas Fotos has resonated is not just the political implications of using folkloric music in addition to música urbana, but its sound. The traditional genres are “a lot more digestible” to listeners who embrace the antiquated taboos surrounding Latin trap and scoff at reggaeton’s sexuality. As a result, the combination of sounds makes for an album that is “popular across generations”, she says.
But it only works because it is “musically really interesting. If it was just traditional music, and that’s only what people cared about, it wouldn’t have done as well as it did,” she says.
“Musically, it is super innovative and makes accessible a lot of these older genres that people in Puerto Rico listen to, but he’s been able to globalise these very local genres in a way that no one else has.”

data.title: Including suppliers in new Hong Kong rules on scaffolding nets ‘overreaching’
data.description:
Hong Kong’s development minister has ruled out expanding an approval system for scaffolding nets to cover suppliers, saying it would amount to government overreach, while the first batch of accredited mesh is expected to be available to contractors next week.
The accreditation of scaffold nets was among measures introduced to enhance fire safety at construction sites following the deadly Tai Po inferno in November.
The government ordered the removal of all netting on about 420 buildings undergoing renovations across Hong Kong last month, citing fire safety after the devastating blaze at Wang Fuk Court claimed 161 lives. The rapid spread of the blaze suggested the use of substandard, non-fire-retardant nets was a contributing factor.
Secretary for Development Bernadette Linn Hon-ho on Saturday dismissed the need to extend the accreditation system to cover net suppliers.
“The materials used in the industry are very diverse. And if our regulations have to cover such [details], would the government’s reach be considered too far?” Linn said on a radio programme.
However, she said she welcomed proposals that the Construction Industry Council should draw up a recommended list of suppliers for the sector to refer to.
The government has also set out new requirements to ensure the materials meet standards before the nets can be installed on building facades. The council is tasked with sourcing and testing the nets under a new tracking system.


The council earlier said that the first batch of about 12,000 new nets could be available as early as January 15. Linn confirmed on Saturday that the new nets were undergoing laboratory testing and those that met the required standard would be distributed to building contractors next week.
She acknowledged that the new requirements could increase costs but maintained that the extra expense was nominal compared with the total cost of a project.
“If we need to take samples on-site, track the whereabouts of the net, and conduct inspections on the net after it arrives in Hong Kong, these are new requirements and naturally incur costs,” Linn said.
“This might make people feel costs will go up too much. Since we … need to comply with the [new] regulations, we need to bear these costs,” she said, referring to concerns that the price of the new accredited nets could be up to twice that of the mesh used previously.
“After all, the cost of purchasing these nets, including the inspection fees, accounts for a very small portion of the overall building maintenance or new building construction projects.”
Linn also said there was no need for an across-the-board switch from bamboo to metal scaffolding, which is more resistant to fire.
“There are some scenarios in which the use of metal scaffolding is not suitable. Thus there will not be a ‘one-size-fits-all’ approach,” she said, citing views from the sector that the city’s narrow streets made placing metal scaffolding difficult, while some old buildings were also not strong enough to support it.

data.title: Taiwan to fortify critical facilities and ramp up ammunition output to ward off PLA pressure
data.description:
Taiwan will double down on strengthening key facilities, including defences against drones, and ramp up ammunition production to withstand growing pressure from the mainland, according to senior officials on the island.
Taiwanese Vice-Premier Cheng Li-chiun said on Thursday that the government would continue to reinforce critical infrastructure and defences against unmanned aerial vehicles.
“Taiwan must be more fully prepared, especially in the face of the possibility of various new forms of grey-zone conflicts, including the anticipated risks of drone intrusion,” Cheng said, according to a statement on Friday from the Executive Yuan, Taiwan’s top administrative body.
She was responding to concerns raised about ongoing military pressure from the mainland as well as fresh doubts about the US’ defence commitment to the island.
In an interview with The New York Times published on Thursday, US President Donald Trump was asked about Taiwan and parallels with last weekend’s US raid on Venezuela.
According to the report, when a reporter noted that Chinese President Xi Jinping regarded “Taiwan as a separatist threat to China”, Trump said: “That’s up to him, what he’s going to be doing.”
Trump sought to draw a sharp distinction between the self-ruled island and Washington’s military action in Venezuela, and also rejected the suggestion that the raid could set precedent for Beijing to take a similar action against Taiwan.
Trump added that he had told the Chinese leader he would be “very unhappy” with any move against the island. Trump also suggested that Beijing would not act while he remained in office, saying he did not believe Xi would attack or choke off Taiwan during his presidency, which runs until 2029.
Beijing sees Taiwan as a part of China to be reunited by force, if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Since William Lai Ching-te took office in Taiwan in 2024, Beijing has intensified military pressure on the island and has branded Lai as an “obstinate separatist”.
In blockade drills around the island last year, the People’s Liberation Army also conducted simulated assaults on the island’s key energy facilities.


In the statement from the Executive Yuan, Cheng said the island had deployed a special police force to critical infrastructure sites in the past year, laying a solid foundation to protect these facilities.
“It is essential to establish joint defence and countermeasure mechanisms in advance and strengthen cybersecurity measures,” she said.
She said she “hopes that all units will develop detailed response plans, implement them in daily operations and further refine them through future drills, so that there will be a more comprehensive resilience preparation plan in the new year”.
The island’s defence ministry would also diversify the supply of military ammunition, with the priority on domestic production, to meet the demand of modern artillery and missile warfare, Taiwan-based Central News Agency reported on Saturday.
The Legislative Yuan has also reportedly asked Defence Minister Wellington Koo Li-hsiung to report on Monday about contingency measures and ammunition procurement plans. A written report has already been delivered to legislators.
Bonnie Glaser, managing director of the Indo-Pacific programme at the Washington-based German Marshall Fund, said Trump’s remarks about Taiwan were similar to others he had made before.
“He does not use especially sharp language when talking about Xi – he wants to preserve his good relationship with China’s leader,” she said.
“But he also wants to make clear that he opposes [the] use of force against Taiwan. China has long factored in the likelihood of US intervention in the event of a PLA military action against Taiwan. Trump’s remarks will not likely alter China’s calculations.”


On Monday, when asked about the possibility of a “decapitation operation” being attempted in Taiwan, deputy defence minister Hsu Szu-chien said “our military is prepared for contingencies and regularly coordinates with related units”.
“We anticipate all kinds of potential emergencies and have been rehearsing and preparing accordingly,” he said.
According to Taiwan’s Archives Access Service database, the island has had a security plan since the 1960s for escorting the leader in the event of a major emergency, terrorist attack or enemy attack on Taiwan.
In 2017, Taiwan ran a drill using helicopters for the first time to simulate the safe evacuation of the leader. It had been conducted with armoured vehicles in the past.
Additional reporting by Xinlu Liang

data.title: Hong Kong Tennis Open: Lorenzo Musetti survives epic Andrey Rublev battle to reach final
data.description:
Lorenzo Musetti is one win away from his first title since 2022 after the Italian beat Andrey Rublev on Saturday to reach the Bank of China Hong Kong Tennis Open final.
The No 1 seed recovered from losing an opening-set tiebreak to win a breathless semi-final 6-7, 7-5, 6-4 in two hours and 43 minutes.
Musetti, who had not advanced beyond the quarter-finals in two previous Hong Kong appearances, will face either Alexander Bublik or Marcos Giron in Sunday’s final.
The 23-year-old blinked first in the opening-set tiebreak. At 2-1 down he twice lost points on his own serve.
Rublev ignored the smattering of Italian fans in a packed Victoria Park Tennis Stadium who began chorusing their idol’s name to ruthlessly move 6-1 in front.

The Russian looked like he felt the world was conspiring against him after aiming one shot wide and another into the top of the net, but he eased forwards to put away a volley and capitalise on his third set point.
Rublev had earlier been in the box seat when he broke Musetti to lead 3-2, but the wheels briefly came off as he lost 11 straight points to see a 4-2 advantage rapidly turn into a 5-2 deficit.
The 28-year-old’s famed temper surfaced as he upended a courtside wooden box that was storing his towel after Musetti broke him back to love in the eighth game. The outburst earned Rublev an official warning.
Musetti missed a break opportunity at the start of the second set, then won four straight points from 15-40 in the fourth game. Rublev dug himself out of a similar hole in the next, refusing to yield at 15-40, then advantage down.
A scorching forehand to save the first of those break points left Musetti rooted to the spot in astonishment.
An epic eighth game on Musetti’s racquet featured six deuces and two break points, then Rublev saved himself with an ace at 30-40 down in the ninth.
But there would be no escapology act two games later when Musetti broke to 15. He had to recover from 15-30 before Rublev failed to pick up his opponent’s drop shot on the first set point.
Rublev suffered a heavy bang on his right elbow after he slipped coming into the net at full tilt during the sixth game of the decider. He had earlier appeared frustrated with the slick court surface and, after holding for 4-3, he dashed off court, probably hunting for new footwear.
He would be broken soon after returning, after Musetti unleashed two huge forehands from 30-30 in the ninth game.
In keeping with the topsy-turvy nature of the contest, Musetti had to stave off three break points, including one at deuce when he was lucklessly foiled after a Rublev shot clipped the net cord and looped over his racquet, before finally unfurling the service winner that ended a fabulous contest.

data.title: Hong Kong education summit to discuss impact of geopolitics on universities
data.description:
The impact of geopolitics on education, including whether American universities can still offer students a positive experience, will be discussed among 2,500 delegates from more than 65 jurisdictions at a major international conference in Hong Kong next month.
The city will host the annual Asia-Pacific Association for International Education (APAIE) Conference and Exhibition two years after Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu vowed in a policy address to promote the “Study in Hong Kong” brand by hosting global education conferences and exhibitions.
The five-day event will take place from February 23 to 27, beginning with a pre-conference day, followed by the three-day summit at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre. It will conclude with campus tours for participants to visit all eight local public universities on the last day.
An Education Bureau spokeswoman told the Post that a Study in Hong Kong booth, jointly set up by eight public universities at the conference venue, would showcase the achievements of the city’s tertiary education sector.
The bureau also hoped the conference would help deepen cooperation with global institutions, explore the further recruitment of students and scholars, and support the signing of different student exchange agreements.
The event is part of the “Study in Hong Kong Week” held from February 23 to March 1. Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin revealed the weeklong event in the fourth annual Principals’ Forum organised by the Post last month, saying the move aimed to showcase the city’s strengths as an international education hub.
The conference boasts a series of activities to promote Hong Kong’s higher education to the world, the bureau said.
A three-day international conference on universities of applied sciences will be held after the APAIE.
In 2023, city leader Lee said in his policy address that Hong Kong would develop universities of applied sciences in a bid to change poor perceptions about vocational education.

During the week, a “wide and intensive” promotion campaign will be conducted via social media, websites, videos, overseas and local media and even on universities’ campuses.
Venky Shankararaman, president of APAIE, said in this year’s prospectus that organisers were expecting 2,500 delegates from more than 65 countries or regions for the annual event in Hong Kong next month.
More than 100 panel discussions will be conducted in the three-day conference, with some of the discussions questioning the attractiveness of the traditional study destinations and the impact of geopolitics on students’ choices of their study places amid the rise of some Southeast Asian countries aiming to become education hubs.
“The global landscape of international education is shifting rapidly. Whilst countries like the US, UK and Canada have traditionally dominated the global landscape for international student mobility, we are now witnessing a decisive shift towards Asia-Pacific and European destinations,” the website said.
One of the plenary sessions will discuss why Hong Kong’s higher education system is a top destination for international students on the second day of the conference.
Two vice presidents of two local public universities, Irwin King Kuo-chin from the Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK) and Albert Chau Wai-lap from Baptist University, will speak in the discussion.
Another panel discussion titled “Can US institutions still offer a positive student experience?” will explore challenges facing American higher education.

“In today’s volatile geopolitical climate and amid shifting leadership and governance philosophies, many are questioning whether US higher education institutions can continue to provide a positive and supportive experience for students from the Asia-Pacific region,” according to the panel discussion’s introduction.
Tang Fei, a lawmaker representing the education constituency, told the Post that holding such a large-scale education conference could certainly boost the city’s image of its higher education.
“In the past, Hong Kong did not join such discussions on the global trend of higher education; now when different countries would like to develop themselves into education hubs, it would certainly help Hong Kong to boost its status,” he said.
He urged authorities to hold similar conferences on basic education given the city’s ambition to develop Hong Kong into an international hub for learning, which was not confined to universities.
CUHK is the lead host of the coming event and one of the 13 founding members of APAIE, while the rest of the seven government-funded institutions are co-hosts.
“CUHK last hosted the APAIE conference in 2013 and we are pleased to host it again in 2026 … the event will bring together educators, administrators and policymakers from around the world to discuss and share best practices in fostering global citizenship and enhancing the quality of higher education,” Dennis Lo Yuk-ming, president of CUHK, wrote in the event’s booklet.
